chapters.title: Mediocrity has robbed many of the zeal to lead an extraordinary life
chapters.text:
In an attempt to highlight the importance of relying on the truth, Jim Collins cited the fate of two companies A&P and Kroger, which were direct rivals, with A&P leading the charge in the 1950s. However, in the 1960s, A&P began to lose its grip on the market, and in contrast, Kroger had successfully transitioned from being a mediocre company to a great one. What separated these retail firms was the ability to face the harsh reality that customers wanted to shop in stores that exude class and taste. On the one hand, Kroger went on to overhaul almost 100% of its existing stores to fit the growing demands of the retail business. On the other hand, A&P ignored the brutal facts before it and stuck to the business model that had served it in the past. The result favored Kroger, which had not run away from the truth.

To maintain relevance, ensure your business meets the current demand of your customers.

Judging from this example, companies that choose to see facts and act on them have always ended up at the top of their industry. More so, over the years, the leaders of these companies have relied on cultures that allowed people to be heard, which, in turn, pushes the truth to the open. To set up this culture, a leader must lead by asking questions and not delivering answers. You should not use your questions to undermine your team’s credibility; instead, these questions should come from a deep urge to understand before providing your insight.

Secondly, the study showed that good-to-great companies had relied on debate sessions where arguments and counterarguments would eventually stir the team towards the most viable decisions. Third, great companies do not hide their failures; they dissect them, learn from them, and move on. Nonetheless, this process does not include finger pointing or name dropping. Lastly, good-to-great companies have learned from the information they’ve gathered. They understand the need for immediate action when they see red flags. But, more importantly, they continue to build their company or brand around simple strategies that anyone could have stumbled upon.

Remember that things are not always as they appear to be… Curiosity creates possibilities and opportunities. ~ Roy T. Bennett

chapters.title: The leadership trait of executives in good-to-great companies is finding self-motivated people to join their team
chapters.text:
The companies under the spotlight in Jim Collins’ study performed remarkably well in the 15 years under review. Some even outperformed well-established companies, like Pepsi and Walt Disney. In fact, many of them rose from the brink of obscurity to the summit of their industry. Yet, surprisingly enough, the individuals who had championed this unprecedented growth — people who steered the ship toward greatness and led with determination in the face of endless challenges — had not featured in tabloids as much as the so-called celebrity CEOs.

These leaders acknowledged that they were lucky to have the best teams and the best environment. In other words, they are selfless individuals who believe that the company’s ambition comes before theirs. Therefore, they did not mind taking the back seat when accolades were presented because their service was all about one thing — doing everything they could to help the company win.

The best-performing CEOs’ penchant for success meant that they put the right people in the right position.

This responsibility had driven them to make hard decisions, they did not shy away from transformations, and at the end of it all, they found ways to sustain the company’s success. Take Darwin E. Smith, for instance; he was the CEO that championed Kimberly-Clark’s transit from 36% below the general market stock rating to cumulative stock returns that were 4.1 times that of the general market. In the process, Kimberly-Clark outperformed industry rivals like Procter & Gamble and Scott Paper. Yet, only a few today know anything about Smith’s triumphs because he was nothing like the egocentric CEOs, whom we and boards of directors have come to identify as performance-inducing individuals.

Furthermore, Jim Collins found that, contrary to popular belief, the best-performing CEOs are not necessarily the ones outsourced. On the contrary, the majority of the executives who have led the tide-changing process of transforming good companies into great ones have risen to the position of CEO from within the ranks of the company. In addition, they had set up capable successors who would sustain the success they had kick-started.

chapters.title: Building a great company leads to an extraordinary life because you get to spend your time with people you love
chapters.text:
The wrong people for a company can’t do it any good; they will only drag it down. We often expect that companies need first to have a purpose and then bring people on board afterward to move from good to great. But in Jim Collins’ research, he discovered that the best approach is first to get the right people on board and let go of the wrong people. Then, figure out the direction their company will take. The right people are always self-motivated because their goal is to be part of the company’s success stories. Hence, as a leader, you won’t have to be under unnecessary pressure to manage your workers tightly. On the other hand, having the wrong people on your team is a dream killer because even if you had the right vision, you wouldn’t have a great company.

It is irrelevant to have a great vision without the right people to run with you.

People often think that good-to-great companies are toxic places to work; if you don’t have the necessary skills, your days are numbered. Good-to-great companies are not ruthless, firing people to make their lives difficult. Rather, they are rigorous, ensuring the best people are in the best positions. Here are some disciplines good-to-great companies follow:
• If you are not sure a person is right for your company, don’t hire them; keep searching.
• When you know a person is wrong for the team, act immediately.
• Learn to put the right people in your biggest opportunities.

Did you know? According to the BBC, Amazon was listed as the world’s most valuable public company in 2019.

chapters.title: The good-to-great companies are those that dared to confront a brutal fact
chapters.text:
In an attempt to highlight the importance of relying on the truth, Jim Collins cited the fate of two companies A&P and Kroger, which were direct rivals, with A&P leading the charge in the 1950s. However, in the 1960s, A&P began to lose its grip on the market, and in contrast, Kroger had successfully transitioned from being a mediocre company to a great one. What separated these retail firms was the ability to face the harsh reality that customers wanted to shop in stores that exude class and taste. On the other hand, A&P ignored the brutal facts before it and stuck to the business model that had served it in the past. The result favored Kroger, which had not run away from the truth.

Judging from this example, companies that choose to see facts and act on them have always ended up at the top of their industry. More so, over the years, the leaders of these companies have relied on cultures that allowed people to be heard, which, in turn, pushes the truth to the open. To set up this culture, a leader must lead by asking questions and not delivering answers. You should not use your questions to undermine your team’s credibility; instead, these questions should come from a deep urge to understand before providing your insight.

Secondly, the study showed that good-to-great companies had relied on debate sessions where arguments and counterarguments would eventually stir the team towards the most viable decisions. Third, great companies do not hide their failures; they dissect them, learn from them, and move on. Nonetheless, this process does not include finger pointing or name dropping. Lastly, good-to-great companies have learned from the information they’ve gathered. They understand the need for immediate action when they see red flags. But, more importantly, they continue to build their company or brand around simple strategies that anyone could have stumbled upon.

Remember that things are not always as they appear to be… Curiosity creates possibilities and opportunities. ~ Roy T. Bennett

chapters.title: Self-discipline and not tyrannical order drive business growth
chapters.text:
There is a correlation between success and discipline. In many of the points discussed in this summary, it is clear that discipline was an integral part of the factors that had contributed to the growth of good-to-great companies. Recall that these companies had decided to hire self-motivated individuals who would do all they could to make things work; money was not their sole motivation. Instead, the desire to be part of something great had propelled them to do their best.

Self-control is the chief element in self-respect, and self-respect is the chief element in courage. ~ Thucydides

In other words, these individuals had self-discipline, and as such, their companies did not need to set up bureaucratic policies to manage their output. Instead, these good-to-great companies invested their time in finding simple business strategies, and they developed policies that would make every decision align with these strategies. Therefore, it was more of a business discipline that won them the race rather than hierarchical propaganda that could stifle growth.

Discipline should also extend to the adoption of new technologies.

It is also not a good reason to join the bandwagon of incorporating an innovation just for the fun of it or just because you fear that you will be left out. You should consider a technology only if it aligns with the simplistic approach that brought you thus far. True to this assertion, Jim Collins found no correlation between technology and the demise or growth of a company. However, this does not downplay the role of technology in business growth. On the contrary, companies that rely solely on technology find it difficult to sustain their successes. The author explains that technology is more of an “accelerator than a creator of momentum.” As such, technology would never take the place of great management principles.

chapters.title: You must endure the buildup before you experience the breakthrough
chapters.text:
Just as technology would not take the place of good leadership, acquisition and mergers have little say in the transformation from good to great. Instead, many of the acquisitions and mergers recorded in the business world are desperate attempts to skip the work and jump right into reaping the reward. Unfortunately, this overly utilized approach has done little to establish the sort of success that the parties involved had promised. Two mediocre entities can never come together to form a great company.

For your company to grow into a great company, you need a lot of patience.

In contrast, good-to-great companies that had partaken in one form of acquisition or the other did so only when they had discovered a business strategy that would inform them on the sort of company to acquire. However, before deciding to test the acquisition brouhaha, they must have established an identity. They would have created enough momentum to make it almost impossible for the deal to falter.

The creation of momentum is what Jim Collins called the buildup. The buildup is an ongoing process that takes into account the step-by-step growth inspired by every decision, every business restructuring, every leadership remodeling, and every technology incorporation. In the end, this buildup eventually results in the breakthrough, which is precisely the point during a transition that attracts media hype.

Sustaining this momentum is crucial as many companies that had once shown signs of greatness often failed to make a mark. Why is this the case? It turned out that many companies are tempted to look for quick fixes rather than wait for their effort and ideas to hatch into deliverables. More so, the buildup period does not have a particular time cycle. For example, for some of the companies highlighted in this summary, the buildup to the transition point was three years, while one company had to endure as long as ten years of steady growth before it could crack the ceiling.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
There are many misconceptions surrounding the capacity of a handful of companies or individuals to dominate their industries. Some believe that they are privy to more information or that technology gave them an unfair advantage. However, the brutal truth is that these entities have learned to focus on simple business values. Just ensure you love what you’re doing and you have the skills needed to contribute to the company’s overall success. Be part of those who share the values and standards of your organization. It is impossible to care about your job and believe in its vision without desiring to be part of its success.

Your life will change as you work toward having a great company. It is also impossible to have a great life unless it is purposeful. A meaningful life demands significant work. But in the end, you will be excited that you have contributed to something worthwhile and that your time on earth wasn’t a waste. If you’re not a business person but want to be part of something great, you can channel your energy into something else. You can be involved in making your community, class, or even your church great. But don’t be part of something for selfish reasons; do it because you’re passionate about it and want to see it reach the highest heights possible. Doing this sets you on the path toward becoming a truly great leader, a person who combines extreme humility with professionalism.

Try this

• Cultivate characters and business strategies that define great leaders.
• Invest more in people.
• Find structure in chaos, and subject all your efforts to areas of business that you can dominate.
